explicit_text: Mr. Hibiya was the managing editor of the newspaper who encouraged me to write my stories as a novel rather than history, fearing that the historical account might offend some relations. He understood the smallness of Japanese society and wanted me to avoid being put in a bad position. Unfortunately, my arthritis made it difficult for me to write as he suggested.

explicit_text: I was born in Japan, specifically in Okayama Prefecture, in 1883. My early life involved moving to Tokyo where my father had contracts with ministries like the Army, Navy, and Justice. Japan was a difficult place economically at the time, which led many, including myself, to seek better opportunities abroad, like in America around 1900.

explicit_text: The Foreign Ministry of Japan was involved in sending medals to people like Mr. Mihara, who then sold them here in America. explicit_text: The Emperor of Japan was the figure to whom recommendations for medals were made. The Emperor's role was significant in awarding honors, which were highly valued among the Japanese community here.

explicit_text: Mr. Mihara, who was president of the Japanese Association for 30 years, built a villa on Mercer Island. This place was notable because he did not work but held a prominent position in the community. Mercer Island was part of the Northwest region where people were very generous and kind.

explicit_text: The Japanese Association here has been led by Mr. Mihara for about 30 years, though the president does not receive a salary. Only the secretary gets paid, around $375. explicit_text: I am originally from Okayama Prefecture, born there in 1883. Many people from Okayama came to America, and when I arrived, I found many from my prefecture in Portland and other places. It was a common origin for many Japanese immigrants in the Pacific Northwest and West Coast.

explicit_text: I lived in Tokyo from ages 5 to 9 because my father had contracts to build government ministry buildings there. I attended grade school in Tokyo and studied Chinese literature under a principal who also taught at Aoyama Gakuin High School. Later, my sister stayed in Tokyo and married there, while I returned to our native town after four years.

explicit_text: After leaving junior high, I attended a temple school run by the Nichiren sect for five years. This school was for apprentices of temples who studied Chinese literature and trained to become monks. I was a student there, not an apprentice, and experienced strict discipline including being hit with bamboo whips.

explicit_text: At the temple school I attended, apprentices came from various prefectures including Kagawa. Along with students from Hyogo, Tottori, Hiroshima, and Shimane, I was part of a group of 18 who studied and were disciplined strictly. This showed the wide regional reach of the temple training system.

explicit_text: I came to the United States in 1900 at age 17 aboard the Izumi-Maru, which had been used during the Russo-Japanese War. Upon arrival, I landed first in Victoria, British Columbia, underwent sterilization, and then traveled to Seattle. I worked various jobs across the West Coast, including in Portland, Sacramento, and Idaho, struggling at first but gradually finding my way.

explicit_text: I came to America aboard the Izumi-Maru on March 23, 1900, when I was 17 years old. This ship had been used to block Port Arthur during the Russo-Japanese War. The passengers were mostly poor immigrants with limited education, and the journey was harsh, with people treated almost like cargo.

The treatment there was harsh and dehumanizing. After that, we took a ship from Victoria to Seattle to continue our journey into the United States.

I stayed there for a while after arriving and worked various jobs including on the railroad and in nearby La Grande thinning sugar beets. Portland was a hub for Japanese immigrants in the Northwest.

explicit_text: I spent two to three months in Pendleton, Oregon, after arriving in America. It was part of my early work experience before moving on to jobs in La Grande and other places. Pendleton was one of the stops in my journey to find steady work as a young immigrant.

explicit_text: In La Grande, about eight miles from Portland, I worked thinning sugar beets for a couple of months. We slept on hay upstairs in a barn and were not allowed to smoke, though I didn't smoke anyway. This was one of my first agricultural jobs in America.

explicit_text: I traveled to San Francisco in June 1901 with a railroad gang before moving on to Sacramento. San Francisco was part of my journey through the West Coast as I sought work and community. It was a stepping stone in my early years in America.

explicit_text: I stayed in Sacramento for a while, working as a schoolboy and porter. I lived at the Sambo Hotel in Chinatown, which was a boarding house where people could stay until they earned enough to pay. I also attended English classes at the Buddhist Church on 4th Street, where a man named Kageyama taught me.
